coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence carbon dot as a sensor for detecting of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria: Hydrothermal synthesis of magnetic hollow NiFe2O4-carbon dots nanocomposite material
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmadian-Fard-Fini, Shahla
Ghanbari, Davood
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: The purpose of this research is synthesis of photoluminescence nanoparticles for detection of Pseudomonas aeruginosa that is feasible for bio medial applications. Carbon nano-templates were prepared from soot by burning of almond as a green precursor. Then NiFe2O4 nanoparticles were synthesized on the carbon templates. For preparation of hollow structures, the product was calcinated for removing of carbon templates. Finally, carbon dots were synthesized on the hollow nickel ferrite cores by applying ethylene diamine and citric acid. Phase of the products was approved by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) depict the morphology and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) probe the grain size and hollow structures of the nickel ferrite, carbon dot and nanocomposites. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy approved the purity of the nanostructures. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption and photo-luminescence (PL) spectroscopy confirmed appropriate photo-luminescence under ultraviolet irradiation. Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) shows ferromagnetic property of the both hollow NiFe2O4 and nickel ferrite-carbon nanocomposite. This work presents a new nanostructure as green prepared photoluminescence sensor for detecting of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria, lead and mercury ions. Outcomes confirm decreasing in photoluminescence intensity of the nanocomposite by enhancing of Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Pb(II) and Hg(II) ions. Antibacterial effect of NiFe2O4-carbon dot on the degradation of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria was also examined.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
P. Aeruginosa bacteria
Nanostructures
Magnetic
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NiFe2O4-carbon quantum dot nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of preparation of carbon dot and hollow magnetic nanocomposite.
XRD pattern of carbon dot nanoparticles.
XRD pattern of carbon dot-NiFe2O4 nanocomposite.
Carbon dots on the black tissue under UV light.
SEM images of carbon dots nanoparticles prepared from soot.
SEM images of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles synthesized by hydrothermal method.
SEM images of carbon dot nanoparticles prepared by ethylene diamine-citric acid.
SEM images of nickel ferrite-carbon dot nanocomposite.
TEM images of carbon dot nanoparticles.
TEM images of ferrite nanoparticles before calcination.
TEM images of hollow nanocomposites.
EDX spectroscopy of (a) C@NiFe2O4 (b) hollow NiFe2O4 (c) NiFe2O4-carbon dot.
FT-IR curve of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles.
FT-IR curve of NiFe2O4-carbon nanocomposite.
VSM curve of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles.
VSM curve of NiFe2O4-carbon dot nanocomposite.
UV–Vis spectrum of the carbon dot nanocomposite.
Quenching of PL intensity by addition of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria.
PL intensity of (a) pure carbon dot (b) by addition of Pb(II) and (c) Hg(II) ions.
Antibacterial effect of NiFe2O4-carbon dot on Pseudomonas bacteria.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence carbon dot as a sensor for detecting of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria: Hydrothermal synthesis of magnetic hollow NiFe2O4-carbon dots nanocomposite material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Science, Arak University of Technology, Arak, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The purpose of this research is synthesis of photoluminescence nanoparticles for detection of Pseudomonas aeruginosa that is feasible for bio medial applications. Carbon nano-templates were prepared from soot by burning of almond as a green precursor. Then NiFe2O4 nanoparticles were synthesized on the carbon templates. For preparation of hollow structures, the product was calcinated for removing of carbon templates. Finally, carbon dots were synthesized on the hollow nickel ferrite cores by applying ethylene diamine and citric acid. Phase of the products was approved by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) depict the morphology and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) probe the grain size and hollow structures of the nickel ferrite, carbon dot and nanocomposites. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy approved the purity of the nanostructures. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption and photo-luminescence (PL) spectroscopy confirmed appropriate photo-luminescence under ultraviolet irradiation. Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) shows ferromagnetic property of the both hollow NiFe2O4 and nickel ferrite-carbon nanocomposite. This work presents a new nanostructure as green prepared photoluminescence sensor for detecting of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria, lead and mercury ions. Outcomes confirm decreasing in photoluminescence intensity of the nanocomposite by enhancing of Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Pb(II) and Hg(II) ions. Antibacterial effect of NiFe2O4-carbon dot on the degradation of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria was also examined.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are newly discovered nano-engineered materials that represent an important class of carbon nano structures that have sp2/sp3 carbons (mix of graphitic carbon functionalized with polar carboxyl or hydroxyl groups). In comparison to fluorescent chalcogenide quantum dots CDs are favorable alternatives due to their optical properties, chemical permanence and low toxicity. The ultrafine dimensions and spherical structural has provided a new platform for nano carbon emitters as an alternative to fullerenes or carbon nano tubes [1–3]. Major properties of C-dots are electron donors-acceptors, high quality, inexpensive nature, down- and up-converted PL, photo-catalyst, size dependent absorption, light-emitting range from blue to red, broad excitation spectra, simple functionalization, biocompatibility, solubility in polar solvents, photo-stable against photo-bleaching and photo-blinking. C-dots exhibit nanosecond lifetimes and two-photonic cross-sections similar to those of quantum dots. CDs luminescence may arise from excitons of carbon, emissive traps quantum-confinement, aromatic structures with oxygen containing groups and edge defects [4–6]. CDs are fragments of graphene sheets, which have turned into a new class of fluorescent due to their exceptional electronic properties in bio-imaging and biomedicines [1–6]. Fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals are used for serving as probes in cell imaging and other biomedical applications. There have been a number of experiments in the literature on CDs for fluorescence labeling to replacing of conventional semiconductors. More effective surface passivation has been correlated with brighter fluorescence emissions [1,7–9].

In comparison to other methods like arc discharge, ion beam radiation, oxidation and microwave methods, our procedure is a simple and cost effective method. Bacterial metabolic tests are standard protocols for bacterial identification but usually this process takes time for identification of the bacteria that is not suitable in ill emergency conditions. These technologies require costly instruments for nucleic acid amplification and other special treatments. Detection of bacteria is so valuable for identifying disease and avoidance of epidemic [10–14].

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a rod shaped Gram-negative bacteria that can make happen illness in humans. P. aeruginosa is a multidrug resistant pathogen recognized for its presence everywhere at the same time especially in medical instruments, lead to cross-infections in hospitals. so its treatment can be difficult due to its resistance to antibiotics. It grow in low-oxygen atmospheres, thus has colonized in many natural and artificial environments. Its symptoms are inflammation and sepsis. If colonizations happen in critical organs, like lungs and kidneys, it can be critical. Because it thrives on moist surfaces, this bacterium is capable of extensive colonization https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pseudomonas_aeruginosa [15–17].

Recently numerous budgets are spending to treat wastewater of factory and drinking water. Every year about 7 million people die from the consequences of water-related diseases. Nanostructures have been taking great attention for the detecting and removal of pathogens. In this work fluorescent hollow nickel ferrite-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment. PL peak was decreased by increasing amount of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria, Pb(II) and Hg(II) heavy metal ions that show a fascinating nanocomposite for bio sensing application.

Ni(CH3COO)2 4H2O, Fe(NO3)3 9H2O, NaOH, distilled water were prepared from Merck Company. XRD patterns were achieved by X-ray diffractometer (Ni-filtered) CuKα radiation (Philips). SEM images were obtained using a MIRA3 TESCAN instrument. All the chemicals were used as received without further purifications. Room temperature magnetic properties were investigated using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) device, (Meghnatis Danesh Pajouh Co., Iran) in an applied magnetic field sweeping between ± 10000 Oe. For SEM preparation, a thin layer of silver was coated to conductive the sample and prevent charge accumulation. Ultrasonic set-up (Bandeline MS 73), with a transducer (Ti, 20 kHz) with power output of 100 W was applied for sonochemical dispersion.

0.01 mol of ethylene diamine and 0.01 mol of citric acid were added to the water. Then the dispersion was transferred into Teflon lined stainless autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. The final product was soluble in water.

Firstly soot (as a nano template) was prepared from burning of almonds. Then 0.1 g of soot, 0.01 mol of Ni(CH3COO)2 4H2O and 0.02 mol of Fe(NO3)3 9H2O were dissolved in 180 ml of water. 20 ml of NaOH (1M) was added to the solution for adjusting pH to 9. Then the dispersion was transferred into (Teflon) autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h, the black precipitate was centrifuged and was washed by distilled water for 2 times. Finally the product was calcinated at 500 °C for 2 h for removing of carbon template.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NiFe2O4-carbon quantum dot nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 g of hollow nickel ferrite was dispersed in 40 ml of water and then 0.01 mol of citric acid and 0.01 mol of ethylene diamine were added to the solution and the dispersion was transferred into Teflon lined stainless autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. The precipitate was washed by distilled water and ethanol.

Preparation of hollow nickel ferrite-carbon dot from by ethylene diamine-citric acid by applying hydrothermal procedure is schematically depicted in Fig. 1 . Fig. 2 shows the XRD pattern of carbon dots obtained at 180 °C for 6 h that shows agreements to previous works with no major impurity. Fig. 3 illustrates XRD pattern of hollow nickel ferrite-carbon dot product. Pure cubic phase of inverse spinel of nickel iron oxide (JCPDS No. 10–0325, space group: Fd-3m, number: 27) can be observed in this pattern beside some noises related to amorphous carbon dot.

For photoluminescence investigation CDs samples were added to a tissue and were inserted under ultraviolet irradiation. Typing word (Nano Technology) is brown under ambient light; however, it is appropriately observable under UV irradiation (Fig. 4 ). Explanation on the surface ligands is ambiguous yet and information of the organic passivation are not sufficient to help understanding of the surface states beneficial for light emission. In carbonization stage the precursors change to carbon residues via pyrolysis, dehydration, or distillation. In surface passivation, an insulating shell on the surface minimizes the impact of surface-defect, trap, quenching from the surrounding, and enhance the fluorescence. In functionalization step chemical (biological) reactivity is added on the surface [1].

Fig. 5 illustrates scanning electron microscopy image of carbon dot nanoparticles prepared from (soot) burning of almond green precursor that approve formation of mono-disperse nanoparticles, according to images average particle size is less than 30 nm. Fig. 6 depicts SEM images of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles prepared at 180C for 5 h that confirm synthesis of mono-disperse nanoparticles (the average size is about 50 nm).
Fig. 7 show SEM of carbon dots prepared by ethylene diamine-citric acid by applying hydrothermal method (180 °C for 6 h). Results approve the mediocre particle size is around 30 nm.

Fig. 8 depict SEM of NiFe2O4-carbon dots that were synthesized by ethylene diamine–citric acid at autoclave 180 °C for 6 h. As the scanning electron microscopy confirms size of the nanocomposite are less than 40 nm. These images also approve agglomerated product were produced but size of the carbon dots are less than 30 nm.

SEM is not appropriate for small nanoparticles and because of this reason, transmission electron microscopy images were applied in the following. Fig. 9 illustrate TEM images of carbon dots prepared at 180 °C for 6 h. TEM images of the obtained product show that these CDs are uniform in size ranging around 40 nm in diameter. CDs stacking to form bigger particles. This happens because at higher temperature most of smallest particles stack and form larger particles with inhomogeneous sizes [11–14].
TEM images of nickel ferrite nanoparticles without calcinations are shown in Fig. 10 , results approve formation of nanoparticles with average diameter size less than 40 nm.
Fig. 11 show TEM images of the hollow nickel ferrite-carbon dot, it confirms formation of porous and hollow nano-spheres that are uniform and size ranging about 20 nm in diameter.

Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy of C@NiFe2O4, hollow NiFe2O4 and NiFe2O4-carbon dot are illustrated in Fig. 12 a–c respectively. Kα of nickel, iron and oxygen are observed in the spectra, also Kβ and Lα of nickel and iron are shown in the all figures. The results appropriately confirm presence of Kα of carbon in Fig.s. 12a and c and show obvious increase in the peak intensity related to presence of carbon dot in Fig. 12c.

Fig. 13 depicts FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized hollow nickel ferrite, absorptions around 590 cm−1 are related to the Ni-O and Fe-O bonds and broad peak at 3427 cm−1 is corresponding to O-H bond. Fig. 14 illustrate spectrum of the NiFe2O4-CDs nanocomposite, peak at 1167 cm−1 is attributed to presence of C-O group. Absorption at 2924 and 3430 cm−1 is related to C-H and O-H bonds respectively, absorption at 1642 is corresponding to C=O bonds. All absorptions confirm presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl group on the surface of CDs (functional group).

Vibrating sample magnetometer instrument was applied for studying of room temperature magnetic properties of samples. Hysteresis loops of magnetic hollow NiFe2O4 nanoparticles and hollow NiFe2O4-carbon nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 15 and Fig. 16 respectively. The magnetic nanostructures show suitable magnetizations, which can simply be attracted by a magnet, making them suitable for sensor application. The hysteresis loop also indicates that the as-synthesized nanoparticles show ferromagnetic behaviour with saturation magnetization of 27 emu/g and coercivity around 90 Oe. The hysteresis of NiFe2O4-carbon nanocomposite also shows ferromagnetic behaviour with saturation magnetization of 22 emu/g and coercivity about 220Oe.

Magnetic communication between the nickel ferrite surrounded by carbon dots was examined. This mixing goes to enhance of coercivity from 90 Oe to 220 Oe compare to pure hollow nickel ferrite nanoparticles. Seemingly, magnetic domains are attached by carbon dots and therefore higher magnetic field is required for altering in domains.

UV–Vis spectrum of carbon dots prepared by citric acid-ethylene diamine is depicted in Fig. 17 . The carbon dots shows absorption bands around 350 nm which is attributed to π-π* transition of carbon dot.

Carbon dot is suitable candidate as fluorescent probe in bacteria detection. Photo luminescence of carbon dots at 180 °C is shown in Fig. 18 . Interestingly results confirm the quenching of PL by increasing amount of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria. PL intensity around 590 nm was obtained with the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. Decreasing in photo luminescence of carbon dots in the presence of lead(II) and mercury(II) ions is illustrated in Fig. 19 . This nanostructure shows a suitable sensor for detecting of toxic and dangerous heavy metal ions. Orbital (d) of Pb2+ can accept the excited electron from CDs more simplicity in comparison to other metal ions. With the addition of lead and mercury ions, the fluorescence of the CDs could be quenched due to the formation of complex between ion(II) and CDs and the occurrence of electron transfer process [18]. In CDs when graphene is cutting along armchair and zigzag edges can be obtained. The PL of CDs are depended to surface/edge state, precursor, quantum confinement, size, defect, passivation and recombination [1–3].

A fluorescence emission in the visible is due to photogenerated electrons and holes trapped at surface sites and their associated radiative recombination. Carbon dots show higher quantum yield and stability as they show fluorescence after six months. Quantum yield (φ) is estimated from Eq.: φ = φr × Ar/Ir × I/A × n2/nr 2, is the quantum yield of Rhodamin B is φr (reference about 0.31). Ar (A) and Ir (I) symbolize absorption and integral area under the curve for reference and sample respectively. Quantum yield for prepared carbon dots was estimated about 20% [1,4–6].

Fluorescence at various concentrations of CDs-labeled Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria were analyzed. This results show that amount of concentration of these bacteria at this range can be measured. Quantum confinement change CDs to non-zero band gap that has PL with excitation. Addition of bacteria to CDs solution leads to surface changes and decreasing or quenching of PL intensity [1,18–21].

Antibacterial effect of NiFe2O4-carbon dot nanocomposite on the degradation of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria is shown in Fig. 20 . As time increase, more bacteria are degraded on the nanoparticles, until obvious circle was observed around antibacterial NiFe2O4-carbon dot disk [22–26].

Carbon dots are interesting materials that are currently attracting world-wide scientific and technological interest because of their unique optical properties. Carbon dots with high quantum yield were synthesized on the nickel ferrite magnetic cores in the presence of ethylene diamine and citric acid. These prepared carbon Dots show suitable fluorescence stability under UV irradiation. A rapid and sensitive method for quantitative detection of Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria using carbon dots as a novel fluorescent sensor was introduced. The quantum dots modified with natural extracts were prepared by a facile method and its quantum yield was about 20%. This proposed method was easy in operation with high sensitivity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/6576766).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal green synthesis of magnetic Fe3O4-carbon dots by lemon and grape fruit extracts and as a photoluminescence sensor for detecting of E. coli bacteria

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. Sharon and M. Sharon, Graphene: an introduction to the fundamentals and industrial applications, Wiley, ISBN 978-1-118-84256-0.
